sections.paragraph:
not used in Kal, to breathe, to blow, especially with the nostrils (an onomatopoet. root, like the cogn. פּוּחַ to blow, especially with the mouth, and נוּחַ prop. to respire. Arab. رَاحَ to rest, prop. to respire, fich verfchnauben. In the Germanic languages, in the same sense is the old root huch, hugh; whence Allem. hugi, Swed. hugh = רוּחַ spirit, Germ. hauchen).

Hiphil הֵרִיחַ (riechen), to smell (as is done by drawing the air in and out through the nostrils, durch Einund Ausziehn der Luft), followed by an acc. Gen 8:21 27:27 . Metaph. for to feel (fire brought near), Jud. 16:9 to presage (as a horse, the battle, which, however, in fact, is through scent), Job 39:25.-Followed by בְּ to smell with pleasure, to be pleased with the smell of any thing, Exo 30:38 Lev 26:31 hence, generally, to delight in, Amo 5:21 Isa 11:3, הֲרִיחֹו בְּיִרְאַת יְיָ “his delight shall be in the fear of Jehovah.” The signification of a sweet smell is often applied to that which pleases, with which we are delighted, see בָּשַׂם , נִיחֹחַ .

Derivatives, רֵיחַ, and pr.n. יְרִיחֹו, and especially-
